feedback.text: Global tech giants erode common decency standards in order to gain slivers of revenue percentiles. These are often large tradeoffs for individual privacy, choice, convenience and utility. I understand there is work being done to: make it as easy to decline all cookies as it is to accept, to unsubscribe as it is to subscribe and other sensible common sense initiatives around digital gambling etc. I encourage the EU to go further. Cancelled subscriptions should last for the period paid for, rather than til the date of cancellation (if you have paid for a month, you should get a months service). There should be no penalties for unsubscribing until the company is no longer receiving funds. Mobile app Advertisements have become misleading and are rarely withdrawn after reports, in-game currencies provide little benefit to the user other than to add a layer of mental separation between game purchases and "real" money. Random purchases should list the chances of each result as players can be fooled into thinking there is a greater likelihood of winning than there actually is. Social media algorithms operate on interacted content, *not* enjoyable content, leading to increased social and culture divides. These algorithms and their quick dissolve into extremist or hateful content should be reviewed even before the addictive nature is addressed. Customizable "Loop breaks" or reminders to consider taking a break may be a useful requirement for social apps for those trying to become less addicted. Walled garden initiatives in Mobile phone devices are reaching to quell competition, hobbyist developers and content deemed undesirable by corporations rather than law. This has also been seen lately with large e-commerce providers Visa & MasterCard who have an effective monopoly, withdrawing service to perfectly legal adult content. I encourage the EU to look into neutrality of service laws, much how Internet service providers cannot be held liable for their customers' websites, mobile phone and e-commerce platforms should not be a arbitrator of their customers' behaviors, especially when it is on their own inconsistently-applied whims and not legally backing. The EU has done great work in keeping the digital world an open, free and fair place and I encouraged further development in the manner done in the past - sensible, measured and effective. Thank you for reading
